text: The surgical team performs an apical lymph node dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery laparoscopically. They medially dissect the IMA pedicle to its origin, skeletonizing it to facilitate potential ligation. Using an energy device, they divide the proximal dissection and enter the plane between the vascular sheath and tunica adventitia. Vessels crossing this plane are managed with diathermy. The left colic artery is identified, and surrounding fat is transected to aid dissection. The superior rectal artery is isolated for later transection.

The inferior mesenteric vein is visible, indicating the extent of dissection along the mesentery. The surgeon proceeds parallel to the proximal inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to reach the prepared proximal dissection extent. The specimen is transected at this level and removed for pathological evaluation. The anatomy is clearly visible, including the superior rectal artery, proximal IMA, left colic artery, and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The surgeon divides the superior rectal artery, completing the specimen preparation.

The surgeon identifies a sigmoidal branch from the left colic artery, known as the descending branch, and divides it to prevent bleeding or compromise to the left colic artery. The inferior mesenteric vein is then divided, and the superior rectal artery is transected. A silk tie is used to measure the distance between the aorta and the left colic artery.

Using a robotic approach, the surgeon begins with a medial dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) origin. The dissection proceeds between the vascular sheath and tunica adventitia, avoiding direct grasping of the vessels to prevent bleeding. Vessels crossing this plane are controlled with diathermy bursts.

The surgeon carefully dissects distally along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to identify the left colic artery. Upon identification, the surrounding fat is transected while avoiding injury to the left colic artery, sigmoidal branches, or the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The dissection continues until the IMV is clearly visible, marking the limit of the dissection.

The surgeon proceeds parallel to the proximal inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to reach the previously identified proximal extent of dissection. Care is taken to avoid injuring the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) more proximally. The specimen is transected for separate evaluation. The anatomy is clearly demonstrated with the surgeon holding the IMV, while the superior rectal artery is being divided, and the left colic artery is preserved. This marks the final specimen.

This patient has undergone long-course chemoradiotherapy and therefore the total lymph node yield is low. However, as you can see, approximately ten percent of lymph nodes are present in the proximal IMA. Here we have come to the end of our video presentation. Thank you.
